turns.student: What brings you in today?
turns.patient: I've had a persistent cough for about 5 days now, and it's just not going away. It started out dry but has been producing yellowish-green sputum lately, and I'm getting really worried about it.

turns.student: Can you tell me more about what you've been feeling?
turns.patient: It's just a really annoying cough, to be honest. It hurts my chest when I talk or laugh, and sometimes when I breathe in deeply. I'm also feeling pretty tired and sluggish, which is unusual for me because I usually have a lot of energy. And to make matters worse, I've missed three days of work already, which is really affecting my job as an elementary school teacher – I love working with the kids, but now I just feel like I'm not doing them justice.

turns.student: When you have those episodes, do you notice anything else happening to your body?
turns.patient: When I have these episodes of coughing and wheezing, I also get a bit short of breath. It feels like my lungs are working harder than usual to take in air, and it's really uncomfortable.

turns.student: Any sweating or clamminess when the symptoms hit?
turns.patient: Yes, actually, just last night I woke up with night sweats - I was drenched through my pajamas for the fourth night in a row. It's been really uncomfortable and unsettling, to be honest.

turns.student: So no perspiration at all during the episodes?
turns.patient: Actually, just the opposite - night sweats have been happening every night for 4 nights now, but not during the day while I'm experiencing symptoms.

turns.student: Does the discomfort stay in one spot, or does it move around?
turns.patient: It seems to be centered mostly in my chest and upper back when I cough or breathe deeply. Sometimes, after a particularly bad episode of coughing, my whole body feels achy and tired, but that usually subsides once I've rested for a bit.

turns.student: Does it radiate anywhere — like your arms, neck, or jaw?
turns.patient: To be honest, when I'm having a bad episode of coughing, my shoulders and upper back feel really tight and sore. Sometimes it feels like it's spreading to my neck, but that's usually after I've been coughing for a while.

turns.student: Any tingling or numbness in your arms when it happens?
turns.patient: No, I haven't experienced any tingling or numbness in my arms during these episodes.

turns.student: What about your extremities — any tingling, pain, or unusual sensations?
turns.patient: No, like I said, I don't get any tingling or numbness in my arms. But to be honest, when I've been feeling really under the weather and exhausted from this cough, sometimes my hands feel a bit cold to the touch, but that's about it.

turns.student: Have you noticed any swelling anywhere? Ankles, feet, hands?
turns.patient: No, I haven't noticed any swelling in my ankles, feet, or hands. To be honest, I'm more worried about the cough and how it's affecting my daily life and work right now, but I do have some swelling in my neck after a particularly bad episode of coughing, it feels like a bit of puffiness under my skin, but that usually goes away once I've rested for a while.

turns.student: Do your shoes feel tighter than usual, or any puffiness in your legs?
turns.patient: No, to be honest, I haven't really noticed anything with my shoes feeling tighter or having puffiness in my legs. To be fair, I've been so focused on this cough and how it's affecting my work that I'm not really paying attention to those kinds of details right now.

turns.student: Any stomach issues during the episodes? Nausea, queasiness, upset stomach?
turns.patient: Actually, yeah - sometimes when I have a bad episode of coughing and wheezing, my stomach feels kind of queasy afterwards. It's not usually severe nausea or anything that would keep me from eating, but it's definitely there. And to be honest, I've also been taking OTC dextromethorphan for the past week with minimal relief, which has been really frustrating because I was hoping it would help knock this cough out of me by now.

turns.student: Do you feel sick to your stomach when the symptoms come on?
turns.patient: Yes, sometimes when I have a bad episode of coughing and wheezing, my stomach feels kind of queasy afterwards. It's not usually severe nausea or anything that would keep me from eating, but it's definitely there.

turns.student: Have you had any fevers, chills, or night sweats?
turns.patient: Yes, actually, I have been experiencing a low-grade fever - around 38.2°C (100.8°F) - that seems to be intermittent and worse in the evenings. And like I mentioned earlier, I've also had some pretty severe night sweats, drenching through my pajamas every night for the past four nights now. It's been really concerning for me, especially since I'm not usually someone who gets fevers or has issues with sweating.

turns.student: Have you noticed any changes in your appetite or weight?
turns.patient: To be honest, yes, my appetite has actually decreased over the past 5 days. I just haven't felt like eating much of anything lately, and when I do, it's not very satisfying.

turns.student: Tell me about your lifestyle — diet, exercise, daily routine.
turns.patient:
I'm an elementary school teacher, so my job is pretty demanding. I'm usually up early to get to work by 8 am, and then I have a long day of teaching and grading papers. When I get home, I try to unwind with some reading or watching TV before bed, but lately, I've been feeling too tired for that. I love my job, though - the kids are always so full of energy and enthusiasm, it's infectious! But now, I just feel like I'm not doing them justice because I'm so exhausted all the time.

As for diet, I try to eat pretty healthily, but with work being so busy, it can be tough. My partner and I usually take turns cooking dinner, and we stick to simple meals - pasta, stir-fries, that kind of thing. I've been trying to stay hydrated by drinking plenty of water throughout the day, but even that's been a struggle lately because my throat is always sore from coughing.

Exercise-wise, I'm not super active, but I do try to get in some walking during recess when I'm at school. It's not much, but it's better than nothing! And over the weekends, we like to take short walks around our neighborhood or go for a hike if the weather permits. I've been meaning to start taking some yoga classes to help with stress and flexibility, but that's still on my to-do list.

As for daily routine, it's pretty standard - wake up early, get to work, come home, relax, bed. But lately, that's been disrupted by this cough and all the fatigue that comes with it. I've been trying to take naps during the day when I can, but even those don't seem to be helping much anymore.

turns.student: How active are you physically? Do you work out or walk regularly?
turns.patient: To be honest, I'm not super active. As an elementary school teacher, my job is pretty demanding and most of my physical activity comes from running around after the kids during recess. I try to get in some walking then, but it's not really enough to call it a workout. On weekends, my partner and I might go for a short walk around our neighborhood or take a hike if the weather permits, but that's about it.

turns.student: What does a typical day of eating look like for you?
turns.patient: My diet has been pretty standard lately. My partner and I usually take turns cooking dinner, so we stick to simple meals like pasta, stir-fries, or omelets. We try to eat healthy, but with work being so busy, it's not always easy. Breakfast is usually something quick like toast or cereal, and lunch might be a sandwich or leftovers from the night before. I've actually noticed that my appetite has decreased over the past 5 days, so even when I do eat, it doesn't feel very satisfying.

turns.student: Any medical problems in your family? Parents, siblings?
turns.patient: My mom has asthma, and she's been managing it with medication for years. That's really about it - nothing else that comes to mind right now.

turns.student: Specifically, has anyone in your family had serious diseases?
turns.patient: Yes, my mom has asthma, which she's been dealing with for a while now. She's been managing it pretty well with medication, but I'm worried that maybe there's something similar going on with me? To be honest, it's making me a bit anxious to think about the possibility of having something like that too...
